primary_diagnosis.name: Acute symptomatic xerostomia flare in secondary Sjogren's syndrome (friction dysphagia); food-bolus impaction/angioedema excluded clinically

comorbidities.name: Secondary Sjogren's syndrome with rheumatoid arthritis overlap

pathophysiology_summary: Secondary Sjogren's syndrome (here overlapping mild rheumatoid arthritis) causes autoimmune destruction of exocrine glands with dry eyes and mouth; profound xerostomia leaves too little saliva to lubricate a food bolus, so dry/crumbly food adheres to the mucosa causing burning, gagging and a choking sensation. This mimics an oesophageal food-bolus impaction and acute angioedema, but the ability to swallow water and saliva with a normal voice, no drooling and no lip/tongue swelling excludes a true bolus obstruction, airway compromise and anaphylaxis — all assessable over video. Streptococcal pharyngitis (fever, exudate, tender nodes) is also excluded. Management is aggressive lubrication (artificial saliva), avoiding dry foods, and a bolus/angioedema/pharyngitis safety-net.

differential.evidence_basis: Dry-food-triggered friction dysphagia with severe xerostomia on known secondary Sjogren's.
Screen for inability to swallow saliva/drooling; absent.
Afebrile, no exudate or tender nodes.

Swallows water/saliva with normal voice and no drooling (DI-001), no lip/tongue swelling or wheeze (DI-002), dry-food trigger on known secondary Sjogren's, afebrile (DI-003) — friction dysphagia from xerostomia.
Swallows water/saliva without drooling — excluded (safety-net retained).
No lip/tongue swelling, wheeze or breathing difficulty.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: artificial saliva before meals, avoid dry foods, sugar-free gum, routine rheumatology review, and a bolus/angioedema/pharyngitis safety-net. Ambulance/ED over-triage for a patient swallowing water with a normal voice is unnecessary.

red_flags.description: True food-bolus impaction/airway compromise — inability to swallow saliva, drooling, or voice change/stridor
Anaphylactic angioedema — lip/tongue swelling, wheeze, or breathing difficulty

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Escalating friction dysphagia to ED despite the patient swallowing water with a normal voice and no swelling.
Reassuring without screening for inability to swallow saliva/drooling or lip/tongue swelling.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Endoscopy if a true bolus is suspected
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Artificial saliva + avoid dry foods + sugar-free gum, with a bolus/angioedema/pharyngitis safety-net; routine rheumatology review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Over-triaging friction dysphagia wastes resources; missing a true bolus/angioedema would be dangerous (hence the retained safety-net).
